text: ball. Gavin is, undisputed by everyone, the cutest boy in high school. Not in tenth grade, but in the entire student body . This is the first time I’ve ever been asked out, and it’s Gavin Bowers. Any girl would be absolutely stupid over his invitation. Except I can’t allow myself to feel that way, or say yes, or even smile at his words, because Della liked Gavin Bowers first, and she likes him. A lot. But as far as I know, Gavin has never even sent a card to Della since she’s been out of school. That should say everything that needs said. That first fleeting elation in my gut is chased away by an unshakable feeling of Ewwww. He can’t get my sister so he’s trying his luck with me , the cheaper, knockoff copy. Right there in front of everyone, waiting for the school buses to line up, I start yelling at him. It comes out so quickly, I’m not sure what I say, but it cuts him to his core, because he slinks away into the crowd. A teacher I don’t know stares at me, but doesn’t say anything. Word must have spread among the staff, and I guess when your sister is dying, you get away with a lot. I’m still full of rage as I reach the top of the stairs and walk into our bedroom, ready to spew about what an asshole Gavin is, but Mom’s there, sitting on Della’s bed and spoon-feeding her chicken soup. My anger evaporates. I put my backpack on the floor, lie down on my bed, and watch them. “Pepperoni pizza for dinner tonight.” Mom’s voice is fake enthusiasm. I groan inside. It’ll be the third time in the last week we’ve gotten pizza from Sal’s. Someone baked us a deep-dish lasagna a couple days ago, and I’ve had enough red sauce to last me a while, but I keep quiet. Della’s eyes are barely open, but she keeps opening her mouth and accepting food like a drowsy baby bird. Mom’s quick hand follows up after each spoonful, dabbing a napkin on Della’s chin. I hear the spoon scraping the bowl and watch Mom deliver the last of it. She sighs and stands and glances at me before she leaves the room. Her footsteps sound abnormally heavy as she descends downstairs. I watch Della’s sleepy eyes, the hint of a smile as her gaze finds me, and then it grows smaller and distant, like bubbles rising to the surface of water. I’m not going to tell Della about Gavin. I realize there’s no point. # Della has sent me on a top-secret mission. I take this request very seriously, though given the circumstances, I don’t think our mother would complain much about taking one of her special chocolate truffles for Della. I’ve already made up my mind to steal two of them instead of the one Della asked me to get for her. She needs something to smile about. I hear my mother talking on the phone, and as I reach the bottom of the steps, I see the shadows of movement in the living room. “They just don’t know, Janice. Test after goddamned test, and I… Oh, I know, I know. Making money out the ass and can’t figure out what’s wrong.” Her voice drops in tone, worn and weary, and for a split second, I almost feel guilty taking the chocolate truffles, but it’s only for a split second. I make my way into the kitchen and pause in front of the refrigerator. In my mind, red laser beams crisscross in front of me in a deadly crosshatch pattern, daring me to try to get past them. I take a deep breath, exhale slowly, and blindly feel around on top of the fridge until I touch the round tin hidden up there. I drag it closer, bring it down with both hands, and pop the lid off. Inside, I see a variety of spheres wrapped in beautiful red, blue, and golden foil. I take two of the gold ones, pause to wonder if Mom counts the number of colors, and decide to take one gold and one blue. I slide the tin back on top of the fridge, careful not to touch one of the red laser beams and trigger the alarm. Satisfied with the placement, I pocket the truffles and turn, put my back against the wall, and inch toward the stairs again. I make my way to the doorframe, and Mom walks by me quickly, the phone still pressed against her ear. She doesn’t notice me at all. “I don’t know, Janice. I truly don’t, but thank you.” She goes to the kitchen sink, pulls a cooking pot from the drying rack, and then takes a can of chicken noodle soup from the corner cabinet. “No, no. The lasagna was great, but soup is just about all she can keep down anymore.” Mom opens the can of soup and dumps it into the pot. She adds some tap water to the mixture and sets the pot on the counter. “Hang on, Janice.” She puts the phone down and opens the cabinets beneath the sink. I watch her take out a small blue bottle, but her back is to me and though I don’t see what she does with it at the counter, she bends down and puts the bottle away again. Pressed tightly against the wall, my spy fantasy dissolves. Mom puts the pot of soup on the stove, and I shift around the corner and sneak upstairs. The smile on Della’s face when she savors the first truffle is something to behold. # The air in the funeral home feels artificially moist. It’s like mist is getting piped in from some kind of hidden machine that hasn’t been cleaned in a long time and smells like rainwater in an old tire. Flower bouquets bookend the gray casket at the front of the room.
